Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
Title: M01 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: A healthcare-associated infection, or HAI, is an infection a person gets while receiving care in a healthcare facility. HAIs are a serious concern in long-term care because residents often have weakened immune systems due to age or chronic illness, residents live in close proximity to one another, many residents need hands-on assistance with daily care, and shared equipment and common areas create opportunities for germ transmission. As a CNA, you have direct contact with residents throughout your shift. That places you in a unique position to both prevent infections and recognize them early.
Transcript: A healthcare-associated infection, or HAI, is an infection a person gets while receiving care in a healthcare facility. HAIs are a serious concern in long-term care because residents often have weakened immune systems due to age or chronic illness, residents live in close proximity to one another, many residents need hands-on assistance with daily care, and shared equipment and common areas create opportunities for germ transmission. As a CNA, you have direct contact with residents throughout your shift. That places you in a unique position to both prevent infections and recognize them early.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
Title: M01 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration: Every time you wash your hands before and after resident contact, put on gloves before handling body fluids, change linens using proper technique, or report a resident's new symptoms to the nurse, you are practicing infection control. Infection control is not just a set of rules. It is a core part of safe, compassionate care. When you protect residents from infection, you help them stay healthier, more comfortable, and more independent.
Transcript: Every time you wash your hands before and after resident contact, put on gloves before handling body fluids, change linens using proper technique, or report a resident's new symptoms to the nurse, you are practicing infection control. Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
Title: M01 L01 C02C Delivery
Narration: Long-term care residents face unique infection risks. Advanced age weakens the immune response. Chronic conditions such as diabetes, COPD, and heart failure reduce resistance. Indwelling devices like catheters and feeding tubes create entry points for germs. Shared living spaces such as dining rooms, activity areas, and bathrooms are used by many people. Residents with cognitive impairment may not be able to follow hygiene steps, and multiple caregivers mean each contact is a potential transmission point. Knowing these risks helps you stay alert during routine care.
Transcript: Long-term care residents face unique infection risks. Advanced age weakens the immune response. Chronic conditions such as diabetes, COPD, and heart failure reduce resistance. Indwelling devices like catheters and feeding tubes create entry points for germs. Shared living spaces such as dining rooms, activity areas, and bathrooms are used by many people. Residents with cognitive impairment may not be able to follow hygiene steps, and multiple caregivers mean each contact is a potential transmission point. Knowing these risks helps you stay alert during routine care.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Why Infection Control Matters in Long-Term Care
Title: M01 L01 C04A Debrief
Narration: Challenge debrief. Your response has been submitted. Infection prevention starts with the action that breaks the chain of transmission for every resident, not just the ones who look sick. Hand hygiene, done correctly and at the right moments, is that action. The safest response is to hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times. Hand hygiene performed correctly and at the right times is the single most effective way to prevent the spread of infection in long-term care. Hands are the main way germs travel between residents, surfaces, and you, so clean hands before and after every contact stop transmission at its most common point. As a CNA, stay within scope: observe, report, document, and follow the care plan. Resident safety and dignity come first, so report any change of condition promptly to the nurse. Before you continue, review the safest response and your own choice so the reason stays with you on the floor.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: Infections spread through a chain of six connected links. Break any one link and you can stop the infection from spreading. The links are: the infectious agent, the germ such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus that causes disease; the reservoir, where the germ lives and multiplies, such as a person, a contaminated surface, or standing water; the portal of exit, how the germ leaves the reservoir through body fluids, respiratory droplets, or skin contact; the mode of transmission, how the germ travels to a new host by direct contact, droplet, airborne spread, or contaminated objects; the portal of entry, how the germ enters a new host through breaks in skin, mucous membranes, or the respiratory tract; and the susceptible host, a person whose body cannot fight off the germ.
Transcript: Infections spread through a chain of six connected links. Break any one link and you can stop the infection from spreading. The links are: the infectious agent, the germ such as a bacterium, virus, or fungus that causes disease; the reservoir, where the germ lives and multiplies, such as a person, a contaminated surface, or standing water; the portal of exit, how the germ leaves the reservoir through body fluids, respiratory droplets, or skin contact; the mode of transmission, how the germ travels to a new host by direct contact, droplet, airborne spread, or contaminated objects; the portal of entry, how the germ enters a new host through breaks in skin, mucous membranes, or the respiratory tract; and the susceptible host, a person whose body cannot fight off the germ.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration: As a CNA you break the chain every day. You attack the infectious agent through proper cleaning and disinfection. You reduce the reservoir with environmental cleaning and proper waste disposal. You close the portal of exit by covering wounds and handling linen properly. You block the mode of transmission with hand hygiene, PPE, and standard precautions. You protect the portal of entry through skin care and wound protection. And you support the susceptible host with good nutrition, mobility support, and early reporting of changes. Hand hygiene breaks the chain at the most common transmission point and is the single most effective way to prevent infection spread.
Transcript: As a CNA you break the chain every day. You attack the infectious agent through proper cleaning and disinfection. Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Hand Hygiene - Your Most Important Tool
Title: M01 L03 C02A Delivery
Narration: The World Health Organization identifies five key moments when healthcare workers must perform hand hygiene: before touching a resident; before a clean or aseptic procedure such as catheter care; after a body fluid exposure risk such as emptying a urinal; after touching a resident; and after touching a resident's surroundings such as the bed rail or call light. A helpful way to remember this is to picture a safety zone around each resident. Clean hands enter the zone, and clean hands leave the zone.
Transcript: The World Health Organization identifies five key moments when healthcare workers must perform hand hygiene: before touching a resident; before a clean or aseptic procedure such as catheter care; after a body fluid exposure risk such as emptying a urinal; after touching a resident; and after touching a resident's surroundings such as the bed rail or call light. A helpful way to remember this is to picture a safety zone around each resident. Clean hands enter the zone, and clean hands leave the zone.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Lesson Title: Hand Hygiene - Your Most Important Tool
Title: M01 L03 C02B Delivery
Narration: Choose your method based on the situation. Use soap and water when hands are visibly soiled, after using the restroom, and after caring for a resident with C. difficile or norovirus; scrub for at least twenty seconds. Use alcohol-based hand sanitizer when hands are not visibly soiled and between routine resident contacts; cover all surfaces and rub until dry, about twenty seconds. Remember that alcohol-based sanitizer does not work against all germs, so use soap and water when hands are visibly dirty or after caring for residents with certain infections.

Remember that alcohol-based sanitizer does not work against all germs, so use soap and water when hands are visibly dirty or after caring for residents with certain infections.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L04 C02A Delivery
Narration: Standard precautions assume that all body fluids except sweat may contain infectious agents. That means you use protective measures for every resident, every time, not just for residents known to have an infection. Standard precautions include hand hygiene, PPE use based on the task, safe handling of sharps and contaminated items, proper linen handling, and respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette.

That means you use protective measures for every resident, every time, not just for residents known to have an infection. Standard precautions include hand hygiene, PPE use based on the task, safe handling of sharps and contaminated items, proper linen handling, and respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L04 C02B Delivery
Narration: Match your PPE to the task. Routine vital signs usually need no PPE unless your skin may contact fluids. For bathing and perineal care, wear gloves, and add a gown if there is a splashing risk. When emptying a urinal or bedpan, wear gloves, and add a gown and eye protection if splashing is likely. Suctioning requires gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection. Changing soiled linen requires gloves, and a gown if it is saturated. For a resident on droplet precautions, wear gloves, gown, and a surgical mask, with eye protection per facility policy. When in doubt, protect more, not less.

Suctioning requires gloves, gown, mask, and eye protection. Changing soiled linen requires gloves, and a gown if it is saturated. For a resident on droplet precautions, wear gloves, gown, and a surgical mask, with eye protection per facility policy. When in doubt, protect more, not less.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L04 C02C Delivery
Narration: Put PPE on in this order: gown, then mask, then eye protection, then gloves. Take PPE off in this order: gloves, then eye protection, then gown, then mask. The doffing sequence matters because gloves are the most contaminated item, so you remove them first to reduce the risk of touching your face or clothing with contaminated gloves. Always perform hand hygiene after removing all PPE.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L05 C02A Delivery
Narration: Because you see residents more often than any other caregiver, you are often the first to notice changes. Report any of these signs to the licensed nurse: fever, or a temperature above the resident's normal range; a new or worsening cough; a change in sputum color, amount, or odor; a change in urine that is cloudy, foul-smelling, or dark; redness, swelling, warmth, or drainage around a wound or catheter site; a change in mental status such as new confusion, agitation, or lethargy, which in elderly residents may be the first sign of infection; decreased appetite or fluid intake; and new skin changes such as a rash, redness, or warmth.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L05 C02B Delivery
Narration: Report new symptoms or changes from baseline immediately by telling the licensed nurse directly; do not wait for charting time. Report vital signs outside the normal range immediately, giving the numbers and describing what you observed. Report changes you noticed during care before the end of your shift at a minimum, following your facility's reporting process. Remember the boundary: you report, and the nurse assesses. Reporting a concern is never wrong, but failing to report one can be dangerous.

Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L06 C02A Delivery
Narration: A clean environment helps break the chain of infection at the reservoir link. Clean high-touch surfaces regularly, including bed rails, call lights, doorknobs, light switches, and toilet handles. Always clean from clean areas toward dirty areas and from top to bottom. Use facility-approved disinfectants and follow the contact-time instructions on the label. Never mix cleaning chemicals, and dispose of contaminated materials in the appropriate containers.
Transcript: A clean environment helps break the chain of infection at the reservoir link. Module Title: Infection Control and PPE
Title: M01 L06 C02B Delivery
Narration: Handle soiled linen carefully to avoid spreading germs into the air or onto surfaces. Hold soiled linen away from your body. Do not shake soiled linen, because shaking can spread germs into the air. Place soiled linen in the designated linen bags or hampers immediately, and never place soiled linen on the floor. Wash your hands after handling soiled linen, even if you wore gloves.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: Every person living in a long-term care facility has legal rights protected by federal and state law. These rights exist because residents are vulnerable - they depend on others for daily care, and that dependence must never lead to loss of dignity or control. Key rights include: Privacy - The right to private conversations, mail, phone calls, and personal care behind closed doors. Dignity - The right to be treated with respect at all times, regardless of condition or behavior. Choice - The right to make decisions about care, activities, meals, and daily schedule. Grievances - The right to file complaints without fear of retaliation. Informed Consent - The right to understand and agree to treatments and procedures before they happen. Freedom from Restraints - The right to be free from physical or chemical restraints used for staff convenience. Confidentiality - The right to have personal and health information kept private (HIPAA).

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration: Resident rights are not abstract legal concepts. They show up in every shift: Knocking before entering = privacy Offering two meal choices = choice Explaining what you are about to do before doing it = informed consent Closing the curtain during personal care = dignity Never discussing a resident's condition in the hallway = confidentiality As a CNA, you practice resident rights hundreds of times a day, often without thinking about it. This module will help you recognize those moments - and catch the ones you might miss.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L01 C02C Delivery
Narration: Two important protections exist outside the facility: Ombudsman: An independent advocate who investigates complaints, mediates disputes, and ensures resident rights are upheld. Every long-term care facility must post ombudsman contact information. Residents and families can contact the ombudsman confidentially. Resident Council: A group of residents who meet to discuss concerns, make requests, and influence facility policies. Staff may attend only if invited. The council is resident-led and must be supported by the facility.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L01 C02E Delivery
Narration: Residents have the right to make decisions about their end-of-life care through advance directives: Advance Directive: A legal document stating a person's wishes about medical treatment if they become unable to communicate. Durable Power of Attorney for Health Care: Appoints someone to make medical decisions on the resident's behalf. Do Not Resuscitate (DNR) / No Code: An order that CPR will not be performed if the resident's heart or breathing stops. Your role as a CNA: Know each resident's code status before an emergency occurs. Never ignore a DNR order. Report any questions about advance directives to the licensed nurse. Never pressure a resident about their advance directive decisions.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: Abuse is an intentional act - or failure to act - by a caregiver or other person in a relationship involving an expectation of trust, that causes or creates a risk of harm to a person age 60 or older. Abuse is a crime. It includes one or more of the following: Willful causing of injury Unreasonable confinement Intimidation - making someone afraid Punishment Refusal of goods or services needed for physical, mental, or psychological well-being Abuse can happen to any older person, but it most often affects those who: Depend on others for help with daily activities Have no family or friends nearby Have disabilities, memory problems, cognitive impairment, or dementia

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration: The following types of elder abuse are recognized under California law and federal regulations: 1. Verbal Abuse Teasing, profanity, racial slurs, or threats directed at a resident. 2. Physical Abuse Battery (kicking, biting, hitting, shoving, pulling hair) or rough handling during care. 3. Psychological / Emotional Abuse Ridiculing, saying hurtful words, repeatedly ignoring, manipulating, yelling at, or threatening a resident. 4. Involuntary Seclusion Isolating a resident or keeping them from seeing close friends and relatives against their will. 5. Abandonment Leaving a resident unattended or failing to plan for their care. 6. Neglect Failure to provide care that a reasonable person would provide. Examples: not answering the call light, leaving a resident smelling of urine without cleaning or changing them. 7. Sexual Abuse Sexual overtures, innuendos, inappropriate gestures, inappropriate touching, or forcing a resident to watch or participate in sexual acts. 8. Financial Abuse Stealing money, borrowing items, accepting gifts, forging checks, or using a resident's bank accounts, credit cards, or property without permission. Also recognized: 9. Healthcare Fraud - Overcharging, double-billing, falsifying Medicare/Medicaid claims, or charging for care not provided.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L02 C02D Delivery
Narration: Recognizing examples in practice: A CNA calls a resident a racial slur when no one else is in the room. - this is Verbal Abuse. A staff member takes $20 from a resident's nightstand. - this is Financial Abuse. A resident is left in a soiled brief for three hours despite pressing the call light. - this is Neglect. A resident is told she cannot leave her room because "she causes too much trouble." - this is Involuntary Seclusion. A CNA yanks a resident's arm during a transfer. - this is Physical Abuse. A staff member repeatedly tells a resident, "Nobody cares about you." - this is Psychological Abuse.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L02 C02E Delivery
Narration: These terms sound similar but have different meanings: Neglect: A pattern of failing to provide care - such as repeatedly not answering call lights, not feeding residents, or not changing soiled linens. Neglect can be intentional or the result of chronic understaffing. Negligence: A single act or failure to act that a reasonably careful person would not have done (or would have done). Example: Forgetting to lock the wheelchair brakes before a transfer, resulting in a fall. Both are serious. Both must be reported. But neglect typically involves a pattern, while negligence may be a one-time error. Your responsibility: Report both. Do not try to determine which one it is - that is for the investigation to decide.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Lesson Title: Signs, Symptoms, and Evidence of Abuse
Title: M02 L03 C02A Delivery
Narration: You may not witness abuse directly. Instead, you will see its effects. Watch for: Behavioral Signs: New-onset sleeping problems Depression or confusion that was not present before Unexplained weight loss Trauma signs: rocking back and forth, flinching at touch Agitation or violent behavior (new or worsening) Withdrawal from activities the resident previously enjoyed Being overly anxious to please a caregiver Physical Signs: Unexplained bruises, burns, or scars Appearing unkempt - unwashed hair, dirty clothes Bed sores or other preventable conditions Frequent injuries Environmental Signs: Living conditions that are unsafe, unclean, or inadequate Personal hygiene that is consistently lacking Medications not taken properly Private conversations not allowed between the resident and visitors

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Lesson Title: Signs, Symptoms, and Evidence of Abuse
Title: M02 L03 C02B Delivery
Narration: When you observe potential abuse, you must document and report using objective observations: Objective: What you can see, hear, feel, or measure. Examples: "Bruise approximately 3 cm on left forearm, purple-yellow in color." "Resident stated: 'She hit me.'" Subjective: What you think or feel. Example: "I think someone is hurting her." Report and document objective observations. Include the resident's exact words in quotes when possible. Do not include your opinions in the chart - but do share your concerns verbally with the licensed nurse. Never document in a way that identifies a real person by full name, date of birth, or facility in training exercises. All training scenarios in this course use fictional examples only.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L04 C02A Delivery
Narration: In California, all CNAs are mandated reporters. This means: You are legally required to report known or suspected abuse, neglect, or exploitation of an elder or dependent adult. You must report immediately - first to your licensed nurse/supervisor, then follow facility policy for external reporting. You do not need to prove abuse happened. You only need reasonable suspicion. Failure to report is a crime. Mandated reporters who fail to report can face fines and criminal penalties. You are protected by law. Reports made in good faith cannot be used against you by your employer. What triggers a report: You witness abuse or neglect. A resident tells you about abuse. You observe signs that suggest abuse may have occurred. A coworker tells you about abuse. What does NOT excuse you from reporting: "I wasn't sure." "I didn't want to get anyone in trouble." "My supervisor said not to worry about it." "It only happened once."

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L04 C02B Delivery
Narration: Step 1: You observe or learn about possible abuse. Step 2: Ensure the resident is safe and not in immediate danger. If they are, call for help immediately. Step 3: Report to the licensed nurse or charge nurse on duty. Step 4: Document your objective observations. Step 5: Follow your facility's policy for completing an incident report or abuse reporting form (e.g., SOC 341). Step 6: If your supervisor does not act, you may report directly to Adult Protective Services (APS), the Long-Term Care Ombudsman, or the Bureau of Medi-Cal Fraud and Elder Abuse. You are not responsible for investigating. You are responsible for reporting what you observed.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L04 C02C Delivery
Narration: Consider this fictional situation: During your evening shift, you enter the room of a fictional resident, "Mr. Rivera" (age 78, mild dementia). You notice a large bruise on his left wrist that was not there at the start of your shift. Mr. Rivera says, "That aide grabbed me hard when I wouldn't get out of bed." He looks frightened. What to remember: Your first priorities are resident safety, immediate reporting to the licensed nurse, and objective documentation. You are a mandated reporter - time matters.

You notice a large bruise on his left wrist that was not there at the start of your shift. Mr. Rivera says, "That aide grabbed me hard when I wouldn't get out of bed." He looks frightened. What to remember: Your first priorities are resident safety, immediate reporting to the licensed nurse, and objective documentation. You are a mandated reporter - time matters.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L05 C02A Delivery
Narration: Abuse thrives in environments with poor oversight, high stress, and inadequate training. As a CNA, you can help prevent abuse by: Recognizing your own stress triggers. Long shifts, difficult residents, and personal problems can lower your patience. Know when to ask for help. Using effective monitoring systems. Answer call lights promptly. Check on residents regularly. Document what you observe. Following policies and procedures. Know your facility's abuse-prevention policies and follow them consistently. Encouraging family visits and social connections. Isolated residents are more vulnerable. Speaking up early. If you see a coworker losing patience with a resident, say something before the situation escalates. Taking care of yourself. Burnout and compassion fatigue are real risk factors for abuse. Use support resources your employer provides.

Module Title: Resident Rights, Abuse Prevention, and Boundaries
Title: M02 L05 C02B Delivery
Narration: Professional boundaries protect both the resident and you: Do not accept gifts from residents or their families. Do not share personal information about your finances, relationships, or problems with residents. Do not develop romantic or sexual relationships with residents. This is always abuse. Do not use your position to gain financial, emotional, or social benefits from a resident. Do maintain respectful, warm, professional relationships that support the resident's care plan. Boundary violations are a form of abuse - even if the resident initiated the contact. Residents are in a vulnerable position, and you are in a position of trust.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: Dementia is not a single disease. It is a group of symptoms affecting memory, thinking, and social abilities severely enough to interfere with daily life. Dementia involves damage to brain cells that affects communication between cells. Key facts: Dementia is not a normal part of aging. Many older adults never develop dementia. Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause, accounting for 60-80% of cases. Other causes include vascular dementia (after strokes), Lewy body dementia, and frontotemporal dementia. Dementia is progressive - symptoms worsen over time. There is currently no cure, but symptoms can sometimes be managed. Important distinction - Delirium vs. Dementia: Delirium is sudden confusion caused by illness, infection (such as UTI), medication changes, or dehydration. It is often reversible with treatment. Dementia develops gradually and is not reversible. If a resident who has been stable suddenly becomes confused, this may be delirium, not worsening dementia. Report any sudden change in mental status to the licensed nurse immediately.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration: Residents with dementia may display behaviors that are challenging but always have a reason - even if the reason is not immediately obvious. Common behavioral symptoms include: Agitation: Restlessness, pacing, yelling, or refusing care. Often triggered by pain, overstimulation, unfamiliar environments, or unmet needs. Sundowning (Sundowner Syndrome): Increased confusion, agitation, or anxiety in the late afternoon or evening. May be related to fatigue, low light, or disrupted internal clocks. Catastrophic Reactions: Sudden, intense emotional responses (crying, screaming, striking out) triggered by frustration, confusion, or sensory overload. Elopement: Leaving the facility without authorization. Residents with dementia may wander due to confusion, restlessness, or trying to go "home." Hallucinations and Delusions: Seeing or hearing things that are not there (hallucinations) or believing things that are not true (delusions). Common in certain types of dementia. Repetitive Behaviors: Asking the same question repeatedly, repeating motions. The resident genuinely does not remember asking. Your response: Stay calm. Do not argue. Do not take it personally. The behavior is the disease, not the person.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L01 C02C Delivery
Narration: Alzheimer's disease typically progresses through three general stages: Early Stage: Forgetfulness (especially recent events) Difficulty finding words Misplacing objects Getting lost in familiar places May still perform most ADLs independently Middle Stage: Increasing memory loss and confusion Difficulty recognizing family and friends Problems with dressing, bathing, and toileting Behavioral changes: agitation, suspicion, wandering May need assistance with most ADLs Late Stage: Severe memory loss - may not recognize self or loved ones Loss of ability to communicate Loss of ability to walk, sit, or swallow Total dependence on caregivers for all ADLs Increased vulnerability to infections (pneumonia, UTI) Your role changes with each stage. In early stages, encourage independence. In middle stages, provide structured support. In late stages, focus on comfort, dignity, and safety.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L01 C02D Delivery
Narration: Dementia is not the only condition that affects cognition. You may also care for residents with: Parkinson's Disease: A progressive nervous system disorder causing tremors, stiffness, and slow movement. Dementia may develop in later stages. Cerebral Vascular Accident (CVA / Stroke): May cause paralysis, speech difficulty (aphasia), and cognitive changes depending on which area of the brain is affected. Intellectual Disability / Developmental Disability: Lifelong conditions present from birth or early childhood. Residents need individualized care plans. Mental Illness: Conditions such as bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, depression, anxiety, or PTSD. These require specific communication approaches and may require medication management by licensed staff. Epilepsy: A seizure disorder. Know each resident's seizure protocol and keep the environment safe. For all conditions: Follow the individualized care plan. Report any changes. Treat every resident with dignity and respect.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: Effective communication with residents who have cognitive impairment requires you to adjust your approach: Approach from the front. Make eye contact before speaking. Use the resident's name. It helps orient them. Speak slowly, clearly, and in short sentences. One idea at a time. Use a calm, warm tone. Your tone communicates more than your words. Give one-step directions. Instead of "Get up, go to the bathroom, and wash your hands," say "Let's stand up now." Allow extra time for responses. Do not rush or interrupt. Use nonverbal cues. Gentle touch, pointing, gestures, and facial expressions support understanding. Avoid arguing or correcting. If a resident says it is 1965, do not argue. Redirect instead. Validate emotions, not facts. "You miss your husband. That must be hard." - even if the husband has been gone for years. Use reminiscence. Talking about past experiences can be comforting and meaningful.

Module Title: Dementia, Communication, and Respectful Care
Title: M03 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration: The Best Friends Philosophy of Communication is a care approach that treats every interaction with a person with dementia as you would treat a conversation with a close friend: Be present. Give your full attention. Show genuine interest. Ask about their life, even if you have heard the story before. Use humor gently. Laughter can ease tension and connect people. Be flexible. If one approach is not working, try another. Celebrate the person, not the disease. Focus on what they can do, not what they have lost. Respect their history. Every person had a full life before this diagnosis. This approach does not require extra time - it requires extra intention.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: Body mechanics is the use of correct muscle groups and body positions during activity to prevent injury. As a CNA, you lift, turn, reposition, and transfer residents many times per shift. Using poor body mechanics puts you at risk for serious back, shoulder, and knee injuries. Core principles: Maintain a wide base of support. Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, one foot slightly ahead of the other. Bend at the knees and hips, not the waist. Use your strong leg muscles, not your back. Keep the load close to your body. The farther you hold a weight from your center of gravity, the more strain on your back. Tighten your core. Engage your abdominal muscles before lifting. Pivot - do not twist. Move your feet to change direction. Twisting while carrying weight is the number one cause of back injury. Push rather than pull when possible. Pushing uses larger muscle groups and is easier to control. Know your limits. If a resident or object is too heavy, get help or use a mechanical lift.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration: CNAs have one of the highest injury rates of any profession. Common injuries include: Back injuries - from lifting, transferring, or repositioning without proper body mechanics. Shoulder injuries - from reaching, pulling, or holding residents during transfers. Slips, trips, and falls - from wet floors, cluttered hallways, or improper footwear. Repetitive strain injuries - from repeated motions such as turning residents or making beds. Needlestick injuries - from handling sharps improperly. Exposure to body fluids - from splashes during care. Prevention strategies: Use proper body mechanics every time - not just when someone is watching. Use mechanical lifts for residents who cannot bear weight. Wear non-slip shoes with closed toes. Report wet floors, broken equipment, and cluttered walkways immediately. Follow Standard Precautions for infection control (reviewed in Module 1).

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: Before any transfer, follow these steps: Check the care plan. Know the resident's weight-bearing status, required assistance level, and any transfer restrictions. Explain what you are doing. "Mr. Davis, I'm going to help you move from the bed to the wheelchair now." Lock all wheels. Wheelchair brakes, bed brakes - everything must be locked. Use a gait belt unless contraindicated. Place it snugly around the resident's waist over clothing, not over bare skin or a gown opening. Clear the path. Remove obstacles, ensure adequate space. Position yourself correctly. Feet shoulder-width apart, knees bent, close to the resident. Count together. "On three, we'll stand. One, two, three." Support, do not lift. Guide the resident's movement; encourage them to help as much as they are able.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration: Bed to Wheelchair: Raise the bed to a comfortable working height if adjustable. Lower the side rail on the transfer side. Assist the resident to sit on the edge of the bed. Allow them to sit for a moment to prevent dizziness (orthostatic hypotension). Place the wheelchair at a 45-degree angle to the bed, on the resident's strong side. Lock the brakes. Move footrests out of the way. Apply the gait belt. Stand facing the resident. On the count of three, help the resident stand using their legs. Pivot (do not twist) and lower the resident into the wheelchair. Position footrests. Ensure alignment and comfort. Stand-Pivot Transfer: Used when the resident can bear weight on at least one leg. Follow the same steps as bed-to-wheelchair, with the CNA providing stability during the pivot. Slide Board Transfer: Used when the resident cannot bear weight. A smooth board is placed as a bridge between the bed and wheelchair. The resident slides across with assistance. Requires specific training. Mechanical Lift: Required when the resident cannot bear weight and is too heavy for manual transfer.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L02 C02D Delivery
Narration: Ambulation means walking. Many residents need assistance to walk safely. Assisting with ambulation: Check the care plan for assistance level and equipment needed. Ensure the resident is wearing non-skid footwear. Apply a gait belt. Stand slightly behind and to the side of the resident's weaker side. Walk at the resident's pace - never rush. Encourage the resident to look forward, not down at their feet. If the resident becomes dizzy or unsteady, assist them to the nearest safe seat. Using a walker: Ensure all four legs or wheels touch the ground. The resident should stand inside the walker, not behind it. The walker moves first, then the weaker leg, then the stronger leg. Using a cane: The cane is held on the strong side. The cane and the weak leg move forward together. Then the strong leg steps forward.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Lesson Title: Range of Motion and Restorative Care
Title: M04 L03 C02A Delivery
Narration: Range of motion (ROM) refers to the full extent to which a joint can move. When residents are immobile or inactive, their joints can become stiff and their muscles can shorten - a condition called contracture. Three types of ROM exercises: Active ROM (AROM): The resident performs the exercises independently. The CNA may provide verbal cues and encouragement. Active-Assistive ROM (AAROM): The resident performs the exercises with some physical help from the CNA. Passive ROM (PROM): The CNA moves the resident's joints through the range of motion. Used when the resident cannot move the joint independently. Key rules for all ROM exercises: Follow the care plan. Know which joints and movements are ordered. Support the limb above and below the joint being exercised. Move gently and smoothly - never force a joint past the point of resistance or pain. Watch the resident's face for signs of pain. Perform exercises in a systematic order (head to toe, or proximal to distal). Report any new pain, swelling, or decreased range to the licensed nurse.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Lesson Title: Range of Motion and Restorative Care
Title: M04 L03 C02B Delivery
Narration: Restorative care is a program designed to help residents maintain or improve their functional abilities. The goal is to prevent decline and support the highest level of independence possible. CNA's role in restorative care: Follow the restorative care plan developed by the licensed nurse or therapist. Encourage residents to do as much as they can for themselves during ADLs. Provide cueing and coaching rather than doing tasks for the resident. Document the resident's participation and any changes in ability. Report progress or decline to the licensed nurse. Examples of restorative care: Walking programs (scheduled ambulation with assistance) ROM exercise programs Self-feeding programs (adaptive utensils, setup assistance, cueing) Toileting programs (scheduled toileting to reduce incontinence) Dressing programs (encouraging self-dressing with setup and verbal prompts) Important: Restorative care is not physical therapy or occupational therapy. It is the ongoing maintenance of skills that therapists have established. The CNA carries out the program under the direction of licensed staff.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L04 C02A Delivery
Narration: Falls are the leading cause of injury in long-term care. Understanding risk factors helps you prevent them. Resident-related risk factors: Age 65 or older History of previous falls Impaired mobility or balance Cognitive impairment (dementia, confusion) Medications that cause dizziness or drowsiness (sedatives, blood pressure medications, pain medications) Orthostatic hypotension (drop in blood pressure when standing) Visual impairment Urinary urgency or incontinence (rushing to the bathroom) Weakness or fatigue Inappropriate footwear (loose slippers, socks without grips) Environmental risk factors: Wet or slippery floors Poor lighting Cluttered walkways Bed height too high Missing or broken handrails Wheels not locked on wheelchair, bed, or commode Unfamiliar environment (new admission, room change)

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L04 C02B Delivery
Narration: CNA responsibilities for fall prevention: Answer call lights promptly. Many falls occur when residents try to get up unassisted because help did not come. Keep the environment safe. Clean spills immediately, remove clutter, ensure adequate lighting, keep pathways clear. Lock wheels. Always lock bed wheels, wheelchair brakes, and commode wheels before assisting. Use non-skid footwear. Ensure residents wear shoes or slippers with non-slip soles. Keep essentials within reach. Call light, water, glasses, tissues - the resident should not need to reach or lean dangerously. Assist with transfers. Follow the care plan for assistance level. Use gait belts. Position the bed low. The lowest bed position reduces fall height if the resident gets out without help. Use bed alarms and sensor mats as directed by the care plan. Assist with toileting on a schedule. Residents who rush to the bathroom due to urgency are at high fall risk. Report near-misses. A "near-fall" today may be a fall tomorrow.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L04 C02C Delivery
Narration: If a resident falls: Do not panic. Stay calm and reassure the resident. Do not try to lift the resident immediately. Assess the situation first. Call for help - use the call light or shout for assistance. Check for injury - observe for pain, swelling, deformity, bleeding, or altered consciousness. Do not move the resident if a head, neck, or spinal injury is suspected. Keep the resident warm and comfortable while waiting for help. Report to the licensed nurse immediately - describe what happened, what you observed, and any complaints from the resident. Document your objective observations according to facility policy. Complete an incident report as directed.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L05 C02A Delivery
Narration: Every CNA must know fire safety procedures. Use the RACE and PASS acronyms: RACE - What to Do When There Is a Fire: R = Rescue - Remove anyone in immediate danger from the area. A = Alarm - Pull the fire alarm and call 911. C = Contain - Close doors and windows to contain the fire and smoke. E = Extinguish or Evacuate - Use a fire extinguisher on a small fire, or evacuate if the fire is large or spreading. PASS - How to Use a Fire Extinguisher: P = Pull - Pull the pin. A = Aim - Aim the nozzle at the base of the fire, not the flames. S = Squeeze - Squeeze the handle. S = Sweep - Sweep from side to side at the base of the fire. Key points: Know the location of fire extinguishers, fire alarms, and exits on your unit. Never use elevators during a fire. Horizontal evacuation (moving residents to another section on the same floor) is usually the first step before vertical evacuation (moving to another floor).

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L05 C02C Delivery
Narration: Choking is a life-threatening emergency. CNAs must be prepared to respond. For a conscious choking adult: Ask, "Are you choking? Can you speak?" If the person cannot speak, cough, or breathe, act immediately. Stand behind the person. Place one foot between their feet for stability. Make a fist with one hand. Place it just above the navel, below the sternum. Grasp your fist with your other hand. Deliver quick, upward abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver). Continue until the object is expelled or the person becomes unconscious. For an unconscious choking adult: Lower the person to the floor. Call for help / activate emergency response. Begin CPR (if trained and certified). Look in the mouth before rescue breaths - remove visible objects. Continue until help arrives. For a conscious choking infant (if applicable in your setting): Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts. Important: Know your facility's emergency response codes and procedures. Practice makes response automatic.

Module Title: Mobility, Falls, and Workplace Safety
Title: M04 L05 C02D Delivery
Narration: Disasters can strike at any time: earthquakes, floods, power outages, severe storms, or facility emergencies. Your responsibilities: Know your facility's disaster plan. Where are the exits? Where is the assembly point? What is your role? Know where emergency supplies are stored (flashlights, batteries, first aid kits, water). During an earthquake: Drop, Cover, and Hold On. Stay away from windows. After the shaking stops, check residents for injuries and follow evacuation procedures. During a power outage: Stay calm. Use flashlights (not candles). Check on residents who depend on electrical equipment (oxygen concentrators, electric beds). Report equipment failures to the nurse. During any disaster: Follow your supervisor's instructions. Account for all residents. Stay calm - your demeanor affects the residents.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: The body needs six basic nutrients to function: Carbohydrates - The body's main source of energy. Found in bread, rice, pasta, fruits, and vegetables. Proteins - Build and repair tissues. Found in meat, fish, eggs, dairy, beans, and nuts. Fats - Provide concentrated energy and help absorb vitamins. Found in oils, butter, cheese, and nuts. Vitamins - Support body processes (immune function, vision, blood clotting). Found in fruits, vegetables, dairy, and meat. Minerals - Support bone health, fluid balance, and nerve function. Examples: calcium, potassium, iron, sodium. Water - The most essential nutrient. Makes up about 60% of body weight. Required for every body function. MyPlate (replacing the old food pyramid) recommends: Half your plate: fruits and vegetables One quarter: grains (preferably whole grains) One quarter: protein A serving of dairy on the side Limit sodium, added sugars, and saturated fats

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration: Hydration means maintaining adequate fluid levels in the body. Most adults need approximately 6-8 glasses of fluid per day, unless restricted by the care plan. Dehydration occurs when the body loses more fluid than it takes in. Older adults are at high risk because: Thirst sensation decreases with age. Kidneys become less efficient. Medications may increase fluid loss (diuretics). Cognitive impairment may prevent the resident from asking for or drinking fluids. Fear of incontinence may cause residents to limit fluid intake. Signs of dehydration: Dry mouth and tongue Decreased urine output / dark urine Confusion (new or worsening) Dizziness or lightheadedness Dry, flaky skin / poor skin turgor Rapid heart rate Constipation Low blood pressure Your role: Offer fluids regularly - do not wait for the resident to ask. Track fluid intake if required by the care plan (I&O). Report signs of dehydration immediately. Provide fluids the resident prefers (within dietary restrictions) to encourage intake. Ensure fresh water is always within reach.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L01 C02C Delivery
Narration: Many residents have modified diets prescribed by a physician or dietitian: Regular diet: No restrictions. Mechanical soft: Food is chopped, ground, or mashed. For residents with chewing difficulty. Pureed: Food is blended to a smooth consistency. For residents with severe chewing or swallowing difficulty. Thickened liquids: Liquids are thickened to nectar, honey, or pudding consistency to prevent aspiration. Common in residents with dysphagia (swallowing difficulty). Low sodium: Limits salt. For residents with hypertension, heart failure, or kidney disease. Diabetic / Carbohydrate-controlled: Manages blood sugar levels. Renal diet: Limits potassium, phosphorus, and sometimes protein. For residents with kidney disease. NPO (Nothing by Mouth): No food or drink - usually before a procedure or test. Your responsibilities: Check the diet card or meal ticket before serving. Never give a resident food that is not on their prescribed diet. Report if a resident refuses to eat or is unable to eat. Assist with feeding if ordered (covered in Lesson 5.2).

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: When a resident requires feeding assistance, follow these guidelines: Wash your hands and apply gloves if required by facility policy. Position the resident upright - at least 60-90 degrees. Head of bed elevated, or seated in a chair. Sit at the resident's eye level. Do not stand over them. Identify the food for the resident. Describe what is on the plate. Offer food in small bites. Use the tip of the spoon - about half full. Alternate between foods unless the resident has a preference. Allow adequate time between bites. Do not rush. Offer fluids frequently between bites to help with swallowing. Watch for signs of choking - coughing, gagging, color changes, inability to speak. Document intake. Record percentage of meal consumed and fluid intake as directed.

Describe what is on the plate. Offer food in small bites. Use the tip of the spoon - about half full. Alternate between foods unless the resident has a preference. Allow adequate time between bites. Do not rush. Offer fluids frequently between bites to help with swallowing. Watch for signs of choking - coughing, gagging, color changes, inability to speak. Document intake. Record percentage of meal consumed and fluid intake as directed.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration: Aspiration occurs when food, liquid, or other material enters the airway (trachea) and lungs instead of the stomach. Aspiration can cause pneumonia, which can be fatal in older adults. Risk factors for aspiration: Dysphagia (difficulty swallowing) Dementia or cognitive impairment Stroke (CVA) - especially if affecting the swallowing muscles Decreased consciousness or sedation Poor positioning during meals (lying flat or reclined) Eating too quickly or receiving bites too large Aspiration prevention strategies: Position upright (60-90 degrees) during meals and for at least 30 minutes after. Follow the prescribed diet consistency - if thickened liquids are ordered, never give thin liquids. Give small bites and allow adequate time between them. Watch for coughing, choking, or a wet/gurgling voice during or after eating. Provide oral care before meals - a clean mouth reduces bacterial risk if aspiration occurs. Never feed a resident who is lying flat, drowsy, or unresponsive. Report any new difficulty swallowing to the licensed nurse immediately.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Lesson Title: Skin Integrity and Pressure Injury Prevention
Title: M05 L03 C02A Delivery
Narration: A pressure injury (also called pressure ulcer, bedsore, or decubitus ulcer) is damage to the skin and underlying tissue caused by prolonged pressure, usually over a bony prominence. How it happens: When a resident sits or lies in the same position for too long, the weight of the body compresses the skin and tissue against the bone. This restricts blood flow, depriving the tissue of oxygen and nutrients. Without intervention, the tissue dies. Common locations: Sacrum (tailbone) - most common Heels Hips (greater trochanter) Elbows Shoulder blades Back of the head Ears Ankles Risk factors: Immobility (cannot reposition independently) Incontinence (moisture weakens the skin) Poor nutrition (the body cannot repair itself) Dehydration Advanced age (thinner, more fragile skin) Decreased sensation (diabetes, spinal cord injury) Friction and shearing (from dragging during repositioning)

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Lesson Title: Skin Integrity and Pressure Injury Prevention
Title: M05 L03 C02B Delivery
Narration: Pressure injuries are classified in stages: Stage 1: Intact skin with a localized area of non-blanchable redness. Skin appears red (in lighter skin) or may appear different from surrounding area in darker skin. When you press on it, the redness does not turn white (non-blanchable). Stage 2: Partial-thickness skin loss involving the epidermis and/or dermis. Looks like a shallow open wound, blister, or abrasion. The wound bed is pink or red, without slough (dead tissue). Stage 3: Full-thickness skin loss. Subcutaneous fat may be visible, but bone, tendon, and muscle are not exposed. May include undermining (tunneling under surrounding skin). Stage 4: Full-thickness tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle. High risk for infection. May include slough or eschar (dark, leathery dead tissue). Unstageable: Full-thickness loss where the wound base is covered by slough or eschar and the true depth cannot be determined. Deep Tissue Injury (DTI): Intact skin with a deep bruise-like area that is purple or maroon. Indicates damage below the surface that may worsen rapidly. Your role: Observe and report.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Lesson Title: Skin Integrity and Pressure Injury Prevention
Title: M05 L03 C02D Delivery
Narration: Pressure injury prevention is a core CNA responsibility: Reposition every 2 hours (or per care plan). Alternate: left side, back, right side. Keep skin clean and dry. Change soiled linens and briefs promptly. Use moisture barrier cream as ordered to protect skin from incontinence. Keep linens wrinkle-free. Wrinkles create pressure points. Ensure adequate nutrition and hydration. Report poor intake. Use pressure-relieving devices as ordered: special mattresses, heel protectors, foam wedges. Avoid dragging during repositioning. Lift - do not slide - to prevent friction and shearing. Inspect skin during care. Check bony prominences during bathing, repositioning, and toileting. Report any skin changes immediately - redness, warmth, discoloration, breakdown, or complaints of tenderness. Elevate heels off the bed using pillows or heel protectors per care plan.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L04 C02A Delivery
Narration: Vital signs are measurements that indicate the body's basic functions. They are "vital" because changes can signal serious medical problems. The six vital signs you will monitor: Vital Sign: Normal Adult Range: Method Temperature: 97.0°F - 99.0°F (oral): Oral, tympanic, temporal, axillary, rectal Pulse: 60-100 beats per minute: Radial (wrist) most common Respirations: 12-20 breaths per minute: Count chest rises for 1 full minute Blood Pressure: Systolic: 90-120 mmHg / Diastolic: 60-80 mmHg: Manual or electronic BP cuff Oxygen Saturation (SpO2): 95-100%: Pulse oximeter on finger Pain: 0-10 scale (0 = no pain): Ask the resident to rate their pain Key points: Know each resident's baseline vital signs so you can recognize changes. Report any reading outside the normal range or significantly different from the resident's baseline. Measure vital signs at the time and frequency specified in the care plan. Never estimate or guess a vital sign reading. If you are unsure, retake it.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L04 C02B Delivery
Narration: Temperature measurement methods: Oral: Most common in alert, cooperative residents. Normal: 97.6°F - 99.6°F. Wait 15-30 minutes after hot/cold food or drink. Tympanic (ear): Quick and comfortable. Place gently in the ear canal. Normal: 98.6°F (similar to oral). Temporal (forehead): Non-invasive. Swipe across the forehead. Becoming increasingly common. Axillary (armpit): Least accurate. Normal is about 1 degree lower than oral. Rectal: Most accurate but most invasive. Used only when other methods are not possible. Normal is about 1 degree higher than oral. Report immediately: Temperature above 101°F (oral) or per facility threshold. Temperature below 96°F - hypothermia risk. Any sudden change from the resident's baseline.

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L04 C02C Delivery
Narration: Pulse: The most common site is the radial pulse (thumb side of the wrist). Use your index and middle fingers - never your thumb (it has its own pulse). Count for a full 60 seconds, especially if the rhythm is irregular. Note: rate, rhythm (regular or irregular), and strength (strong, weak, thready, bounding). Report immediately: Pulse below 60 or above 100 (or per facility threshold). Irregular rhythm. Pulse that is weak, thready, or significantly different from baseline. Respirations: Count the number of chest rises in 60 seconds. Observe without telling the resident - awareness may change their breathing pattern. Note: rate, depth (shallow or deep), and pattern (regular or irregular). Report immediately: Respirations below 12 or above 20 per minute. Labored, noisy, or irregular breathing. Use of accessory muscles (neck, shoulders) to breathe. Cheyne-Stokes breathing (covered in Module 3).

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L04 C02D Delivery
Narration: Blood Pressure: Systolic (top number): pressure when the heart beats. Diastolic (bottom number): pressure when the heart rests between beats. Proper cuff size is essential - too small gives a falsely high reading; too large gives a falsely low reading. The resident should be seated or lying down, arm at heart level, relaxed. Do not take BP on an arm with an IV, fistula, or on the affected side after a mastectomy. Report immediately: Systolic above 160 or below 90. Diastolic above 100 or below 60. Significant change from baseline. Symptoms: dizziness, headache, blurred vision, chest pain. Oxygen Saturation (SpO2): Measured with a pulse oximeter on the fingertip. Normal: 95-100%. Remove nail polish or use an earlobe sensor if readings are questionable. Report immediately: SpO2 below 90% - this is a medical emergency. SpO2 below 95% or any drop from baseline. Symptoms: shortness of breath, confusion, cyanosis (blue color around lips or nail beds).

Module Title: Nutrition, Skin Integrity, Vital Signs, and Observation
Title: M05 L05 C02A Delivery
Narration: Intake = all fluids taken in (oral fluids, IV fluids, tube feeding). Output = all fluids leaving the body (urine, emesis, wound drainage, diarrhea). When I&O monitoring is ordered: Measure and record all oral fluid intake in milliliters (mL). Common conversions: 1 cup = 240 mL; 1 carton of milk = 240 mL; 1 bowl of soup = approximately 180 mL. Measure urine output using a graduated container. Do not estimate. Record I&O at the intervals specified in the care plan. Report significant imbalances - much more output than intake (dehydration risk) or much less output than intake (fluid overload risk).

Title: M05 L05 C02B Delivery
Narration: Weight changes can signal nutrition problems, fluid retention, or disease progression. Weigh residents at the same time of day (usually before breakfast), wearing similar clothing, on the same scale. Report any weight loss of 5% or more in 30 days, or 10% or more in 6 months. Report any sudden weight gain (may indicate fluid retention). Document the weight, date, time, and any factors that may affect accuracy.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L01 C02A Delivery
Narration: As a CNA, you spend more time with residents than any other member of the healthcare team. This makes you the most important observer. Observation means using your senses to gather information: Sight: Skin color, swelling, facial expressions, body position, wound appearance, food intake. Hearing: What the resident says, breath sounds, crying, moaning. Touch: Skin temperature, moisture, swelling, pulse. Smell: Odors from wounds, urine, breath, body. Everything you observe should be categorized as either objective or subjective: Objective observations (also called signs): What you can measure, see, hear, feel, or smell. Examples: "Blood pressure 150/95," "Bruise 3 cm on left forearm," "Temperature 101.2°F." Subjective observations (also called symptoms): What the resident tells you. Examples: "I feel dizzy," "My stomach hurts," "I feel like I'm going to throw up." Document and report both. Use the resident's exact words for subjective observations, placed in quotes.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L02 C02A Delivery
Narration: Your documentation is a legal record. It can be used in court, reviewed by surveyors, and referenced by the entire care team. Follow these rules: Document promptly. Record observations as soon as possible after the event. Be objective. Write what happened, not what you think happened. Use measurable terms. Be specific. Instead of "ate well," write "consumed 75% of lunch." Use the resident's words in quotes. "Resident stated: 'My back hurts when I move.'" Use approved abbreviations only. If you are unsure about an abbreviation, write the full word. Never erase, use white-out, or scribble over entries. For paper records, draw a single line through the error, write "error," initial, and date. Sign every entry with your name, title (CNA), date, and time. Never chart in advance. Only document what has already happened. Never chart for someone else. Each CNA documents their own observations. If you didn't document it, it didn't happen. This is a legal principle.
Transcript: Your documentation is a legal record. It can be used in court, reviewed by surveyors, and referenced by the entire care team. Follow these rules: Document promptly. Record observations as soon as possible after the event. Be objective. Write what happened, not what you think happened. Use measurable terms. Be specific. Instead of "ate well," write "consumed 75% of lunch." Use the resident's words in quotes. "Resident stated: 'My back hurts when I move.'" Use approved abbreviations only. If you are unsure about an abbreviation, write the full word. Never erase, use white-out, or scribble over entries. For paper records, draw a single line through the error, write "error," initial, and date. Sign every entry with your name, title (CNA), date, and time. Never chart in advance. Only document what has already happened. Never chart for someone else. Each CNA documents their own observations. If you didn't document it, it didn't happen. This is a legal principle.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Lesson Title: Change of Condition - What to Report
Title: M06 L03 C02A Delivery
Narration: A change of condition is any significant difference in a resident's physical, mental, or emotional status from their normal baseline. Because you see residents every day, you are often the first to notice these changes. Report immediately to the licensed nurse if you observe: Mental status changes: New confusion, disorientation, unusual drowsiness, sudden agitation, unresponsiveness. Vital sign changes: Blood pressure, pulse, temperature, respirations, or SpO2 significantly different from baseline. Pain: New pain, worsening pain, or pain not relieved by current interventions. Skin changes: New redness, bruising, swelling, warmth, rash, skin breakdown, or signs of pressure injury. Fall or injury: Any fall (with or without visible injury), near-fall, or new injury. Respiratory changes: Shortness of breath, difficulty breathing, coughing, wheezing, or change in sputum. Intake changes: Refusing meals, significant decrease in food or fluid intake, difficulty swallowing, choking. Output changes: Decreased urine output, blood in urine or stool, new incontinence, severe diarrhea or constipation, nausea/vomiting. Mobility changes: New weakness, inability to bear weight, unsteady gait, inability to perform ADLs previously performed.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Lesson Title: Change of Condition - What to Report
Title: M06 L03 C02C Delivery
Narration: When you report to the licensed nurse, be organized. A common framework is SBAR: S = Situation: "I'm calling about Mr. Lee in Room 212." B = Background: "He has a history of CHF and was stable this morning." A = Assessment (your observations): "His blood pressure is now 180/105, he is short of breath, and his ankles are more swollen than this morning." R = Recommendation: "I think he needs to be assessed soon." Key points: Report in person or by phone - not by sticky note or text message. Be specific. Use numbers, measurements, and the resident's exact words. Include the time you noticed the change. If the nurse is not available, follow your facility's chain-of-command policy.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L04 C02A Delivery
Narration: Your scope of practice defines what you are legally allowed to do as a CNA. It is determined by state law, your training, and your facility's policies. CNAs typically CAN: Assist with ADLs: bathing, dressing, grooming, feeding, toileting. Measure and record vital signs. Perform ROM exercises as directed. Assist with transfers, ambulation, and positioning. Observe and report changes in condition. Document care provided. Provide comfort measures. Answer call lights. Assist with intake and output measurement. Perform postmortem care as directed. Provide emotional support and companionship.
Transcript: Your scope of practice defines what you are legally allowed to do as a CNA. It is determined by state law, your training, and your facility's policies. Assist with transfers, ambulation, and positioning. Observe and report changes in condition. Document care provided. Provide comfort measures. Answer call lights. Assist with intake and output measurement. Perform postmortem care as directed. Provide emotional support and companionship.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L04 C02B Delivery
Narration: CNAs CANNOT: Administer medications (including over-the-counter). Perform sterile procedures (wound packing, catheter insertion, IV management). Diagnose conditions or make medical judgments. Give medical advice to residents or families. Take verbal or telephone orders from physicians. Assess or evaluate - this is a licensed nurse function. Delegate tasks to other CNAs (delegation flows from licensed nurse to CNA). Perform any task they have not been trained to do, even if asked. Ignore the care plan or modify it without authorization. The critical rule: If you are unsure whether a task is within your scope, ask the licensed nurse before proceeding. Performing tasks outside your scope puts the resident at risk and puts your certification in jeopardy.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L04 C02C Delivery
Narration: If someone asks you to perform a task outside your scope: Do not perform the task. State your scope clearly: "I am not trained or authorized to do that as a CNA." Report the request to the charge nurse or supervisor. Document if needed. Example scenario: A family member says, "Can you just give my mother her pain pill? The nurse hasn't been by yet." Correct response: "I understand your concern. I'm not authorized to administer medications. Let me contact the nurse right away so your mother can get her medication as soon as possible."

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L05 C02A Delivery
Narration: Professionalism as a CNA means: Reliability: Arrive on time, complete your assignments, and follow through on commitments. Appearance: Clean uniform, name badge visible, minimal jewelry, hair secured, closed-toe shoes. Attitude: Positive, respectful, and patient - even during difficult situations. Confidentiality: Never share resident information with unauthorized persons. This includes social media. Accountability: Own your mistakes. Report errors immediately. Do not cover up or blame others. Teamwork: Communicate clearly with all team members. Help coworkers when you can. Report concerns through proper channels. Continuous learning: Stay current with training requirements. Ask questions when you are unsure.

Module Title: Documentation, Reporting, PHI Avoidance, and Scope
Title: M06 L05 C02B Delivery
Narration: Delegation is the process by which a licensed nurse assigns a task to the CNA. Delegation flows in one direction: from the licensed nurse to the CNA. Five rights of delegation: Right task: The task must be within CNA scope and appropriate for the resident's condition. Right circumstances: The situation must be stable and appropriate for CNA-level care. Right person: The CNA must be trained and competent to perform the task. Right supervision: The licensed nurse must be available for questions and oversight. Right direction/communication: The instructions must be clear and specific. Your responsibility when receiving a delegated task: Make sure you understand the instructions. Ask questions if anything is unclear. Perform the task as directed. Report back to the nurse when the task is complete. Report any problems, changes, or concerns immediately. Never accept a task you are not trained or competent to perform. It is your right and responsibility to say no.

Module Title: Final Review, Exam/Test, Affidavit, and Certificate Status
Title: M07 L01 C02B Delivery
Narration:
The chain of infection has six links: infectious agent, reservoir, portal of exit, mode of transmission, portal of entry, susceptible host. Breaking any link stops infection.
Standard Precautions apply to all residents at all times. Treat all blood, body fluids, and non-intact skin as potentially infectious.
Hand hygiene is the single most effective infection prevention measure. Wash for at least 20 seconds with soap and water, or use ABHR when hands are not visibly soiled.
PPE must be donned and doffed in the correct sequence to prevent self-contamination.
Transmission-based precautions (contact, droplet, airborne) add protection above Standard Precautions for residents with known or suspected infectious conditions.
Report signs of infection in any resident to the licensed nurse immediately.

Title: M07 L01 C02E Delivery
Narration:
Every resident has legal rights including privacy, dignity, choice, informed consent, freedom from restraints, and confidentiality.
There are eight primary types of elder abuse: verbal, physical, psychological, involuntary seclusion, abandonment, neglect, sexual, and financial. Healthcare fraud is also recognized.
Neglect = a pattern of failing to provide care. Negligence = a single act of carelessness.
In California, all CNAs are mandated reporters. You must report known or suspected abuse immediately - you do not need certainty, only reasonable suspicion.
The reporting decision path: ensure safety -> report to licensed nurse -> document objectively -> follow facility policy for formal reporting.
Professional boundaries protect residents and staff. Accepting gifts, romantic relationships, and sharing personal financial information all violate boundaries.

Title: M07 L01 C02G Delivery
Narration:
Dementia is not normal aging. Alzheimer's disease is the most common cause. Dementia is progressive and not reversible.
Delirium is sudden confusion - often reversible. Report any sudden change in mental status immediately.
Communication strategies: approach from the front, use the resident's name, speak slowly in short sentences, validate emotions not facts, avoid arguing or correcting, use reminiscence.
The Best Friends philosophy treats every interaction as you would treat a close friend - present, respectful, flexible.
Kubler-Ross five stages of grief: Denial, Anger, Bargaining, Depression, Acceptance. Not everyone goes through all stages or in order.
Signs of approaching death include decreased appetite, mottling, Cheyne-Stokes breathing, cool extremities, and decreased responsiveness.
Hospice = comfort care for life expectancy 6 months or less. Focuses on quality, not cure.
Respect cultural and spiritual practices. Ask - do not assume.

Title: M07 L02 C02B Delivery
Narration:
Body mechanics: Bend at knees/hips (not waist), keep load close, tighten core, pivot do not twist, push rather than pull, know your limits.
Always check the care plan before any transfer. Lock all wheels. Use a gait belt unless contraindicated. Count together before moving.
Two-person transfers require two people - do not attempt alone if the care plan specifies two-person assist.
ROM: Active (resident performs), Active-Assistive (resident + CNA), Passive (CNA performs). Never force past resistance or pain.
Restorative care: Support independence - encourage the resident to do as much as safely possible.
Fall prevention: Answer call lights promptly, lock wheels, non-skid footwear, low bed position, keep essentials in reach.
RACE (fire): Rescue, Alarm, Contain, Extinguish/Evacuate. PASS (extinguisher): Pull, Aim, Squeeze, Sweep.

Title: M07 L02 C02D Delivery
Narration:
Six nutrients: carbohydrates, proteins, fats, vitamins, minerals, water.
Older adults are at high risk for dehydration - decreased thirst sensation, incontinence fear, cognitive impairment, medications.
Never give thin liquids when thickened liquids are ordered. Doing so creates aspiration risk.
Position residents upright (60-90°) during meals and for at least 30 minutes after.
The most common pressure injury site is the sacrum. Reposition every 2 hours.
Stage 1 = non-blanchable redness on intact skin. Stage 2 = shallow wound or blister. Stage 3 = fat visible. Stage 4 = bone/tendon visible.
Vital sign normal ranges: Temperature 97-99F (oral); Pulse 60-100 bpm; Respirations 12-20/min; BP 90-120/60-80 mmHg; SpO2 95-100%.
SpO2 below 90% = medical emergency. Report immediately.

Title: M07 L02 C02F Delivery
Narration:
Objective = what you can see, measure, hear, feel, smell. Subjective = what the resident tells you (document in quotes).
Legal documentation rules: document promptly, be specific, use resident's words in quotes, use approved abbreviations, never erase/white-out, sign every entry, never chart in advance or for someone else.
SBAR (change of condition reporting): Situation, Background, Assessment, Recommendation.
Report changes in mental status, vital signs, pain, skin, mobility, intake/output, breathing, or behavior immediately.
CNA scope: can perform ADLs, vital signs, ROM, transfers, ambulation, observation, documentation, comfort measures. Cannot administer medications, perform sterile procedures, diagnose, or modify the care plan.
Delegation flows from licensed nurse to CNA. Five rights: right task, right circumstances, right person, right supervision, right direction.
When in doubt about scope, ask the licensed nurse before acting.

If you do not pass on the second attempt, you will be contacted by the course administrator. The exam is timed - you have 45 minutes. The timer begins when you click "Start Exam."
Questions are presented one at a time. You can navigate back within the exam. All questions are multiple choice or select-all-that-apply. Read each question carefully. The exam is closed-book during the attempt, but you may review course materials between attempts.
